---
title: "Measles Report"
author: "Ruben Jimenez, Jasmine Cabrera, Cameron An"
format: html
editor: visual
theme: united
embed-resources: true
---

```{r setup}
#| label: libraries
#| code-fold: true
#| message: false

library(tidyverse)
library(ggridges)
library(ggrepel)
library(car)
library(pscl)
library(gt)
library(broom)
library(scales)
library(htmltools)
library(MASS)
library(tidyverse)
library(jsonlite)
library(tidyjson)
```

## Motivation

Measles is a highly contagious viral respiratory disease that continues to affect millions of people worldwide each year, despite major scientific advancements and the widespread availability of the MCV1 vaccine. We aim to explore the socioeconomic and geographic factors that may influence measles infection rates. By examining these patterns, we hope to better understand regional disparities and identify populations that are at higher risk.

## Research Questions

1.  What region of the world is affected most severely by Measles annually? 

2.  What socioeconomic factors have an effect on Measles infection rate? 

    a\) Are these socioeconomic factors correlated with vaccine coverage rate?

## Data

### **WHO Measles Data**

**Data Description:**

The original measles data were obtained from the WHO Provisional Monthly Measles and Rubella Dataset on June 12, 2025, and accessed through the tidytuesdayR package via the tidytuesday repository organized by Jen Richmond and Jon Harmon. Two datasets were provided by the package: cases_year and cases_month, each capturing measles incidence at different levels of time. For instance, the cases_year dataset records data with country-year as the observational unit (e.g. Algeria, 2012), while the cases_month dataset records data with country-year-month (Algeria, January 2012). Together, these datasets cover 194 countries over multiple years and months. 

The data originate from the WHO’s global surveillance and national reporting systems. Although both datasets provide information on measles and rubella cases, this analysis focuses exclusively on measles cases. Despite the availability of effective vaccines, measles-related deaths continue to affect countries worldwide, affecting mainly unvaccinated children. Obtaining this data can help us understand trends in measles incidence and the factors that contribute to regions of elevated risk. Such insights are critical for informing public health strategies and implementing changes aimed at reducing the spread of measles.

**Sources:**

[WHO: World Health Organization](https://immunizationdata.who.int/global?topic=Provisional-measles-and-rubella-data&location=)

**Data:**

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| code-fold: true
cases_month <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2025/2025-06-24/cases_month.csv')
cases_year <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2025/2025-06-24/cases_year.csv')

# create measles data
measles_month <- cases_month |>
  dplyr::select(-c(rubella_clinical, rubella_epi_linked, 
            rubella_lab_confirmed, rubella_total)) |>
  rename("Region" = region,
         "Country" = country, 
         "Country Code" = iso3,
         "Year" = year,
         "Month" = month,
         "Suspected Measles" = measles_suspect,
         "Clinical Measles" = measles_clinical,
         "Epi Measles" = measles_epi_linked,
         "Lab Measles" = measles_lab_confirmed,
         "Total Measles" = measles_total,
         "Discarded" = discarded) |>
  mutate(Date = my(str_c(Month, "-", Year)),
         ASCII = stri_trans_general(Country, "Latin-ASCII")) |>
  dplyr::select(-c(Year, Month)) |>
  relocate(Date)

# create measles data for years
measles_year <- cases_year |>
  select(-c(rubella_clinical, rubella_epi_linked, 
            rubella_lab_confirmed, rubella_total,
            rubella_incidence_rate_per_1000000_total_population)) |>
  rename("Region" = region,
         "Country" = country, 
         "Country Code" = iso3,
         "Year" = year,
         "Total Pop." = total_population,
         "Annualized Pop. 2025" = annualized_population_most_recent_year_only,
         "Total Suspected Cases" = total_suspected_measles_rubella_cases,
         "Total Measles" = measles_total,
         "Total Clinical Measles" = measles_clinical,
         "Total Epi Measles" = measles_epi_linked,
         "Total Lab Measles" = measles_lab_confirmed,
         "Total Measles" = measles_total,
         "Total Measles per Million" = measles_incidence_rate_per_1000000_total_population,
         "Total Discarded" = discarded_cases,
         "Total Discarded per Million" = discarded_non_measles_rubella_cases_per_100000_total_population) |>
  slice(-1)

measles_year |> filter(Country == "Mongolia", Year == 2016)
```

### **REST Countries API Data**

**Data Description:** 

Country-level demographic data was accessed via the Rest Countries API, an open-source database maintained by a volunteer community and updated via pull request on Gitlab. This API is updated irregularly due to the open-source nature. These data contain current information about countries such as population, Gini coefficient, land borders, time zone, regional bloc, human development index, and more. Although the data includes a population density variable, it is a fixed value based on the current population at the time of an update. As a result, we innstead opted to create a population density variable. We used the total land area provided in the API (assuming it goes unchanged) and used the annual population from the WHO data to calculate population density.

**Source:**

[REST Countries API](https://restcountries.com/)

**Data:**

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#| eval: false
world_df <- GET("https://restcountries.com/v4/all?fields=cca3,geolocation,
                area,borders,hdi,gini,government,religion") |> 
  (\(x) rawToChar(x$content))() |> 
  fromJSON() |>
  unnest_wider(c(gini, geolocation, government, religion), "_") |>
  relocate(cca3) |>
  rename("ISO3" = cca3) |>
  arrange(ISO3)

# clean data
world_df <- world_df |>
  mutate(Religion = map_chr(religion_name, ~ paste(.x, collapse = ", ")),
         `Religion Pop.` = map_chr(religion_population, ~ paste(.x, collapse = ", ")),
         `Pct Religious` = map_chr(religion_percentage, ~ paste(.x, collapse = ", "))) |>
  mutate(Religion = str_replace_all(Religion, ",\\s*", ","),
         `Religion Pop.` = str_replace_all(`Religion Pop.`, ",\\s*", ","),
         `Pct Religious` = str_replace_all(`Pct Religious`, ",\\s*", ",")) |>
  separate(Religion,
           into = c("Primary Religion", "Secondary Religion",
                    "Tertiary Religion"),
           sep = ",", fill = "right") |>
  separate(`Religion Pop.`,
           into = c("Primary Religion Pop", "Secondary Religion Pop",
                    "Tertiary Religion Pop"),
           sep = ",", fill = "right") |>
  separate(`Pct Religious`,
           into = c("Primary Pct Religious", "Secondary Pct Religious",
                    "Tertiary Pct Religious"),
           sep = ",", fill = "right") |>
  mutate(across(contains("Pop"),
                ~ as.numeric(str_replace_all(.x, ",", ""))),
         across(contains("Pct"), as.numeric)) |>
  select(-c(religion_name, religion_population, religion_percentage, 
            `Secondary Pct Religious`, `Tertiary Pct Religious`,
            `Secondary Religion Pop`, `Tertiary Religion Pop`,
            `Secondary Religion`, `Tertiary Religion`))

world_df |>
  head(10) |>
  select(-government_leaders) |>
  gt() |> tab_header(title = md("REST API Data")) |>
    cols_label(ISO3 = md("**ISO3**"),
               gini_year = md("**GINI Year**"),
               gini_value = md("**GINI Value**"),
               geolocation_latitude = md("**Latitude**"),
               geolocation_longitude = md("**Longitude**"),
               `Primary Religion` = md("**Primary Religion**"),
               `Primary Religion Pop` = md("**Primary Religion Population**"),
               `Primary Pct Religious` = md("**Primary Pct Religious**"),
               government_type = md("**Government Type**"),
               hdi = md("**Human Development Index**"),
               borders = md("**Border Nations**"),
               area = md("**Land Area**")) |>
  
  # format numbers
  fmt_number(columns = c(`Primary Religion Pop`,
                         area),
             decimals = 0,
             use_seps = TRUE) |>
  fmt_number(columns = -c(`Primary Religion Pop`, 
                          area),
             decimals = 3,
             use_seps = TRUE) 
```

### GDP API

**Data Description:**

**Source:**

**Data:**

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#| eval: false
url <- "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_by_past_and_projected_GDP_(nominal)"

# read in page
page <- read_html(url)

tables <- page |>
  html_elements("table")

# 2010s table
table5 <- tables[[5]] |>
  html_table(fill = TRUE)

# 2020s table
table6 <- tables[[6]] |>
  html_table(fill = TRUE)

# keep important years
table5 <- table5 |>
  dplyr::select(-c(`2010`, `2011`))

table6 <- table6 |>
  dplyr::select(c(`Country / territory`,`2020`, `2021`, `2022`, `2023`, `2024`))

# join data
nominal_gdp <- table5 |>
  full_join(table6, by = "Country / territory")

# rename cols
nominal_gdp <- nominal_gdp |>
  rename(`Country` = `Country / territory`)
```

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#| eval: false
# scrape country codes
codeurl <- "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Comparison_of_alphabetic_country_codes"

# read in page
page1 <- read_html(codeurl)

tables <- page1 |>
  html_elements("table")

# code table
table1 <- tables[[1]] |>
  html_table(fill = TRUE)

codes <- table1 |>
  dplyr::select(c(Country, ISO))

# join code and nominal gdp data
nomgdp_code <- nominal_gdp |>
  left_join(codes, join_by("Country"))

# countries without iso code
countries_missing_iso <- nomgdp_code$Country[is.na(nomgdp_code$ISO)]

# iso codes
missing_iso <- data.frame(
  Country = c(
    "Bahamas", "China", "Democratic Republic of the Congo",
    "Republic of the Congo", "Ivory Coast", "Gambia",
    "Hong Kong", "Ireland", "South Korea",
    "Federated States of Micronesia", "North Macedonia",
    "Russia", "Samoa", "Taiwan", "Timor-Leste", "United Kingdom"),
  ISO_new = c(
    "BHS", "CHN", "COD",
    "COG", "CIV", "GMB",
    "HKG", "IRL", "KOR",
    "FSM", "MKD",
    "RUS", "WSM", "TWN", "TLS", "GBR"),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

# add missing iso
nomgdp_clean <- nomgdp_code |>
  left_join(missing_iso, by = "Country") |>
  mutate(ISO = if_else(is.na(ISO), ISO_new, ISO),
         ISO = if_else(Country == "Kosovo", "XKX", ISO)) |>
  dplyr::select(-ISO_new) 
```

### **Measles Vaccine (1st Dose) Immunization**

**Data Description:** 

The World Health Organization collected global data from 194 countries on measles-containing vaccine first-dose (MCV1) coverage among one year olds. The data spans from 2000 to 2024 and is calculated by dividing the total number of vaccinations administered by the number of children in the target population, which is often estimated using census projections. Anyone who is not protected against measles remains at risk, and according to the CDC, two doses are recommended for the strongest protection. Immunization plays an important role in reducing deaths among children under five. 

**Sources:**

[WHO: World Health Organization (Global Health Observatory)](https://www.who.int/data/gho/data/indicators/indicator-details/GHO/measles-containing-vaccine-first-dose-(mcv1)-immunization-coverage-among-1-year-olds-(-))

[CDC: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention](https://www.cdc.gov/measles/about/index.html)

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#| label: cleaning the mcv dataset
#| message: false
#| eval: false

# read in file
mcv <- read_csv(here::here("data", "mcv1.csv"))

# recode values
mcv <- mcv |> 
  dplyr::select(SpatialDimValueCode, Period, Value) |>
  rename("Country Code" = SpatialDimValueCode,
         "MCV1 Coverage" = Value,
         "MCV1 Year" = Period) 
```

## Final Dataset

**Data Description:**

Using the various datasets above, we joined them using the ISO3/Country Code. This code is unique to each country and is a three letter combination. Every row represents a given a country and its attributes for a given year.

```{r}
#| message: false
#| code-fold: true
measles <- read_csv(here::here("report", "measles_df_clean.csv"))

measles <- measles |>
  rename(`Nominal GDP per Capita` = `Nominal GDP`)

head(measles) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_style(style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
            locations = cells_column_labels()) |>
  
  # commas
  fmt_number(columns = c(`Total Pop.`, Area, `Primary Religion Pop`, `Nominal GDP per Capita`),
             decimals = 0,
             use_seps = TRUE) |>
  
  # decimals
  fmt_number(columns = c(Density),
             decimals = 3,
             use_seps = TRUE)
```

## Data Summaries & Visualizations

### Exploring Research Question 1:

#### **Table 1:** Annual Measles Cases Rates per Million

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#| label: figure 1
library(htmltools)

# visualization
annual_measles_tbl <- measles |>
  filter(!is.na(`Total Measles per Million`),
    !is.na(`Total Pop.`)) |>
  split(~ Year + Region) |>
  map_dfr(function(x) 
    {tibble(Year = first(x$Year),
     Region = first(x$Region),
     `Avg. Measles per Million` = mean(x$`Total Measles per Million`, na.rm = TRUE),
     `Avg. Pop.` = mean(x$`Total Pop.`, na.rm = TRUE))}) |>
  arrange(desc(`Avg. Measles per Million`)) |>
  filter(!is.na(Year)) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = md("Annual Measles Cases Rates per Million")) |>
  tab_footnote(footnote = "Data is arranged is descending order by Average Measles Cases per Million.") |>
  
  # format numbers
  fmt_number(columns = c(`Avg. Pop.`, `Avg. Measles per Million`), use_seps = T) |>
  fmt_number(columns = `Avg. Pop.`, decimals = 0) |>
  
  # labels
  cols_label(Year = md("**Year**"),
             Region = md("**Region**"),
             "Avg. Pop." = md("**Avg. Pop.**"),
             "Avg. Measles per Million" = md("**Avg. Measles per Million**")) |>
  
  # add heat maps
  data_color(columns = `Avg. Measles per Million`,
             fn = col_numeric(palette = c("white", "red"),
                              domain = NULL))

# wrap in scrollable div
browsable(div(style = "max-height:500px; overflow-y:auto;", annual_measles_tbl))
```

Table 1 shows that in 2016, Western Pacific countries had the most average cases per million of any region. This follows for 2015 and 2014 as well. Western Pacific countries averaged almost 384 cases per million in 2016. The average country-level population these years stayed close to 75 million people, which suggests that population size may have an impact on infection rate.

#### **Figure 1:** Average Measles Cases per Million by Region and Year

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#| alt-text: "The Americas do not have data for 2012 and 2013. The heatmap indicates that there was a measles outbreak from 2014-2016 in the Western Pacific region. Notably, the African countries seem to have relatively consistent measles cases over time, with a spike in 2019."

# create plot
measles_heatmap <- measles |>
  group_by(Region, Year) |>
  summarise(mean_cases = mean(`Total Measles per Million`, na.rm = TRUE),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  
  # compute log(1+x) since we 0s
  ggplot(aes(x = Year,
             y = Region,
             fill = log1p(mean_cases))) + 
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  
  # non-default colors
  scale_fill_continuous(palette = c("white", "red")) + 
  guides(fill = guide_colorbar(direction = "horizontal")) +
  
  # non-default theme
  theme_classic() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
        axis.title = element_text(size = 12),
        legend.position = "top") +
  
  # labels and alt text
  labs(title = "Average Measles Cases per Million by Region and Year",
       x = "Year",
       y = "",
       fill = "Log (Measles Cases Per Million +1)",
       caption = "Unlike other regions, the Americas do not have data for 2012 and 2013.") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(min(measles$Year),
                                  max(measles$Year),
                                  by = 2))
measles_heatmap
```

Figure 1 shows that in general, Europe, Eastern Mediterranean, and Africa seem to have the highest consistent number of cases across all years. There does not appear to be a consistent pattern of increase or decrease across years, but periods of elevated cases are scattered across different years and regions. This indicates the presence of scattered outbreaks rather than consistent patterns. Europe and the Eastern Mediterranean have experienced an increase in cases over the past three years, producing some of their largest log(mean cases) during this period. There is also a spike in cases throughout 2018 and 2019, which are most pronounced in Europe, Western Pacific, and Africa. The Americas have the lowest number of cases relative to all other regions, which could stem from socioeconomic factors that will be addressed as part of our second research question.

We also note that the Western Pacific region experienced a measles outbreak starting in 2014 and lasting until 2016. This outbreak yielded the highest number of measles cases over the 2012-2025 period across all regions, which can be confirmed by the previous table. Using this information, we decided to further investigate the year-over-year change in measles cases for the Western Pacific region to quantify trends in outbreak severity.

#### **Table 2:** Annual Change in Measles Cases Rate per Million in the Western Pacific Region

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
# year over year change function
yoy_change <- function(df, region_name) {
  
  # input checks
  required_cols <- c("Year", "Region", "Total Measles per Million")
  
  if (!all(required_cols %in% names(df))) {
    missing_cols <- required_cols[!required_cols %in% names(df)]
    stop(paste("Missing required columns:", paste(missing_cols, collapse = ", ")))}
  
  if (!is.character(region_name) || length(region_name) != 1) {
    stop("Country Name must be a valid country.")}
  
  # case when region is not in dataset (handles capitalization)
  if (!tolower(region_name) %in% c("south east asia", "europe", 
                                    "western pacific", "americas", 
                                    "eastern mediterranean", "africa")){
    stop("Invalid Region Name. Please check spelling. Options are South East Asia, Europe,
         Western Pacific, America, Eastern Mediterranean, and Africa.")}
  
  # make sure first letter is capitalized
  if (tolower(region_name) %in% c("south east asia", "europe", 
                                    "western pacific", "americas", 
                                    "eastern mediterranean", "africa")){
    region_name <- stringr::str_to_title(region_name)}
  
  # empty dataset
  if (nrow(df) == 0) {
    warning("No data found.")}

  # create summary table
    df |>
    filter(!is.na(`Total Measles per Million`),
           !is.na(`Total Pop.`)) |>
    group_by(Year, Region) |>
    summarize(`Avg. Measles per Million` = mean(`Total Measles per Million`, na.rm = T),
              `Avg. Pop.` = mean(`Total Pop.`, na.rm = T),
              .groups = "drop") |>
    filter(Region == region_name) |>
    arrange(Year) |> 
    mutate(`Measles Rate Change` = `Avg. Measles per Million` - 
             lag(`Avg. Measles per Million`)) |>
    dplyr::select(Year, `Avg. Pop.`, `Avg. Measles per Million`, 
           `Measles Rate Change`) |>
    gt() |>
      
    # header
    tab_header(title = md(paste0("Annual Change in Measles Cases Rate per Million in ", 
                                 region_name))) |>
      
    # format numbers
    fmt_number(columns = c(`Avg. Pop.`, `Avg. Measles per Million`, 
                         `Measles Rate Change`), use_seps = T) |>
    fmt_number(columns = `Avg. Pop.`, decimals = 0) |>
      
    # labels
    cols_label(Year = md("**Year**"),
               "Avg. Pop." = md("**Avg. Pop.**"),
               "Avg. Measles per Million" = md("**Avg. Measles per Million**"),
               "Measles Rate Change" = md("**Measles Rate Change**"))} |>
  
  # color
  data_color(columns = `Measles Rate Change`,
             fn = col_numeric(
               palette = c("white", "red"),
               domain = NULL))
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
# western pacific measles rate change
yoy_change(measles, "Western Pacific")
```

Table 2 shows that between 2014 and 2016, the mean number of measles in the Western Pacific countries increased at an alarming rate. The region in 2014 saw an increase of almost 256 average cases per million. 

#### **Figure 2:** Average Measles Infection Rate Across Time and Region

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
# grab label data
plot_data <- measles |>
  filter(!is.na(`Total Measles per Million`),
         !is.na(`Total Pop.`)) |>
  split(~ Year + Region) |>
  map_dfr(function(x) {
    tibble(
      Year = first(x$Year),
      Region = first(x$Region),
      `Avg. Measles per Million` = mean(x$`Total Measles per Million`, na.rm = TRUE),
      `Avg. Pop.` = mean(x$`Total Pop.`, na.rm = TRUE))}) |>
  filter(!is.na(Region))

# grab order and last year for regions
label_data <- plot_data |>
  group_by(Region) |>
  slice_max(Year) |>
  ungroup()

plot_data |>
ggplot(aes(x = Year, 
           y = `Avg. Measles per Million`, 
           color = Region, 
           fill = Region)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point() +
  
  # fixing colors
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Africa" = "#d35e7f",
                                "Americas" = "#9fc7b9",
                                "Eastern Mediterranean" = "#a999eb",
                                "Europe" = "#9bcdef",
                                "South East Asia" = "#ff9d60",
                                "Western Pacific" = "#d4a9ce")) +
  
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Africa" = "#d35e7f",
                               "Americas" = "#9fc7b9",
                               "Eastern Mediterranean" = "#a999eb",
                               "Europe" = "#9bcdef",
                               "South East Asia" = "#ff9d60",
                               "Western Pacific" = "#d4a9ce")) +
  
  # adding labels
  geom_label_repel(data = label_data,
                   aes(x = Year, 
                       y = `Avg. Measles per Million`, 
                       label = Region,
                       fill = Region),
                   inherit.aes = FALSE,
                   nudge_x = 1,    
                   direction = "y",
                   hjust = 0,
                   segment.color = NA) +
  expand_limits(x = max(label_data$Year) + 6) +
  
  # adding theme
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(face = "bold"),
        axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
        legend.position = "none") + 
  labs(title = "Average Number of Measles Cases per Million Across Time and Region",
       y = "")
```

Figure 2 shows substantial variation in measles rates both between regions and over time. In general, Europe, the Eastern Mediterranean, and Africa tend to have the highest measles rates on a consistent year-to-year basis, as they are frequently among the top three regions beginning in 2017, although this pattern is not consistent in every year. The Western Pacific and Africa tend to experience the most volatile measles rates, as evidenced by the large spikes in cases. The Western Pacific region experienced an extreme measles outbreak from 2014 to 2016, along with a smaller spike in cases during 2019. Africa also experienced a large spike in cases during 2019, indicating an outbreak during that period. These large fluctuations suggest that the Western Pacific and Africa regions are more prone to large measles outbreaks rather than gradual trends over time. 

In contrast, the Americas and South East Asia consistently maintain low measles rates, potentially indicating stronger prevention procedures or more stable vaccination coverage. Europe and the Eastern Mediterranean have experienced the most recent spikes in cases and possess the highest rates as of 2025. 

### Exploring Research Question 2:

#### **Figure 3:** Average MCV1 Coverage for One-Year Olds Across Time and Region

```{r}
#| label: mcv1 plot
#| warning: false
#| code-fold: true
#| alt-text: "The average measles vaccine coverage is substantially lower for countries in Africa than other regions. We also see a dip in coverage globally around the start of the Covid pandemic."

# grab label data
label_data <- measles |>
  group_by(Region, Year) |>
  summarise(mean_coverage = mean(`MCV1 Coverage`, na.rm = TRUE),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  drop_na() |>
  group_by(Region) |>
  slice_max(Year) |>
  arrange(mean_coverage)

# make plot
mcv1_plot <- measles |>
  group_by(Region, Year) |>
  summarise(mean_coverage = mean(`MCV1 Coverage`, na.rm = TRUE), 
            .groups = "drop") |>
  
  ggplot(aes(x = Year, 
             y = mean_coverage, 
             color = Region)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point() +
  
  # labels
  labs(title = "Average MCV1 Coverage for 1 Year Olds Across Time and Region",
       subtitle = "Source: WHO",
       y = "",
       x = "Year") +
  
  # unique colors
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Africa" = "#d35e7f",
                                  "Americas" = "#9fc7b9",
                                  "Eastern Mediterranean" = "#a999eb",
                                  "Europe" = "#9bcdef",
                                  "South East Asia" = "#ff9d60",
                                  "Western Pacific" = "#d4a9ce")) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Africa" = "#d35e7f",
                                  "Americas" = "#9fc7b9",
                                  "Eastern Mediterranean" = "#a999eb",
                                  "Europe" = "#9bcdef",
                                  "South East Asia" = "#ff9d60",
                                  "Western Pacific" = "#d4a9ce")) +
  
  scale_y_continuous(labels = \(x) paste0(x, "%")) +
  
  # edit graph  
  theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(face = "bold"),
        legend.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
        axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
        legend.position = "none") +
  
  # move labels
  geom_label_repel(data = label_data,
                  aes(x = Year, y = mean_coverage, 
                      label = Region, fill = Region),
                      inherit.aes = FALSE,
                      nudge_x = 0.5,
                      direction = "y",
                      hjust = 0,
                      segment.color = NA) +
  
  # expand grid
  expand_limits(x = max(label_data$Year) + 5)

mcv1_plot
```

The dip in MCV1 coverage after 2019 across all regions seen in Figure 3 could be a result of the COVID-19 pandemic, a period in which healthcare resources were reallocated to pandemic response rather than measles prevention. This reallocation of resources might explain some of the increase in cases from 2022 and later, as the low MCV1 coverage is associated with a “delayed” impact on the increased measles cases in recent years.

The Africa region consistently has the lowest MCV1 coverage compared to other regions. This difference appears substantial in the plot above and could explain variation in measles rates across regions. However, we do not see a very large difference in measles rates for Africa compared to the Europe and Eastern Mediterranean regions, as shown through the heatmap provided earlier. 

Together, these results suggest that MCV1 coverage could represent an important predictor of measles rate as evidenced by the delayed impact of the measles vaccine shortage during the COVID-19 pandemic. However, the difference in MCV1 coverage in Africa compared to other regions is substantial and not reflected by proportionally large differences in measles infection rates, suggesting that other factors play an important role.

#### **Table 3:** Correlation Table Between GDP (USD), MCV1 Coverage, Total Measles Cases per Million

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| code-fold: true
measles |>
  filter(!is.na(`MCV1 Coverage`),
         !is.na(`Nominal GDP per Capita`),
         !is.na(`Total Measles per Million`)) |>
  split(~ Region) |>
  map_dfr(function(x) {
    tibble(Region = first(x$Region),
           `MCV1 vs GDP` = cor(x$`MCV1 Coverage`,
                                     x$`Nominal GDP per Capita`,
                                     use = "complete.obs"),
           `MCV1 vs Measles` = cor(x$`MCV1 Coverage`,
                                         x$`Total Measles per Million`,
                                         use = "complete.obs"),
           `GDP vs Measles` = cor(x$`Nominal GDP per Capita`,
                                        x$`Total Measles per Million`,
                                        use = "complete.obs"))}) |>
  arrange(Region) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_style(style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
            locations = cells_column_labels()) |>
  tab_header(title = md("**Correlation Table**"),
             subtitle = md("**Using MCV1 Coverage Rate, GDP (USD), and Total Measles Cases per Million Across Region**")) |>
  
  # format values
  fmt_number(columns = c(`MCV1 vs GDP`, `MCV1 vs Measles`, `GDP vs Measles`),
    decimals = 3,
    use_seps = TRUE)
```

Table 3 shows that regions generally exhibit a positive correlation between nominal GDP and MCV1 coverage, although the strength of this relationship varies. The Eastern Mediterranean region possesses the strongest correlation, suggesting that countries in the Eastern Mediterranean may devote more of their economic resources to vaccine coverage compared to other regions. In contrast, Africa possesses the lowest correlation value, indicating no linear relationship between nominal GDP and MCV1 coverage. This suggests that countries with larger nominal GDPs in Africa do not necessarily achieve proportionally higher vaccination coverage compared to lower-GDP countries. Therefore, economic resources alone are not sufficient to improve vaccination rates in Africa, so other factors should be considered.

#### **Figure 4:** MCV1 Coverage vs. Log Nominal GDP per Capita Across Regions

```{r}
#| label: nominal gdp vs. mcv1 coverage
#| code-fold: true
measles |>
  filter(!is.na(`Nominal GDP per Capita`),
         `Nominal GDP per Capita` > 0,
         !is.na(`MCV1 Coverage`)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = log(`Nominal GDP per Capita`),
             y = `MCV1 Coverage`,
             fill = Region)) + 
  geom_hex() + 
  facet_wrap(~Region) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = \(x) paste0(x, "%")) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Africa" = "#d35e7f",
    "Americas" = "#9fc7b9",
    "Eastern Mediterranean" = "#a999eb",
    "Europe" = "#9bcdef",
    "South East Asia" = "#ff9d60",
    "Western Pacific" = "#d4a9ce")) +
  labs(x = "Log Nominal GDP per Capita (USD)",
       y = "",
       title = "MCV1 Coverage vs. Log Nominal GDP per Capita Across Regions") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(legend.position = "none") 
```

In Figure 4 we compare the first dose measles-containing vaccine (MCV1) coverage across regions against the log of nominal GDP per capita. In Africa, countries with varying GDPs exhibit a wide range of MCV1 coverage, showing no clear relationship between GDP and vaccination rate. In the Americas, the GDP range is narrower, and vaccination rates generally remain high. The Eastern Mediterranean region shows a clear pattern, with higher GDP countries tending to have higher MCV1 coverage. In Europe and South East Asia, MCV1 rates remain consistently high regardless of GDP, with South East Asia showing rates of at least 70% for most countries, aside from one outlier.

#### **Table 4:** Nominal GDP per Capita and MCV1 Coverage for the Western Pacific Region

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
mcv_gdp <- function(region = NULL, country = NULL, year = NULL){
  
  # input checks
    # check that at least region or country is provided
  if (is.null(region) & is.null(country)) {
    stop("Please provide at least a region or a country.")}
  
  # valid regions
  valid_regions <- c("South East Asia", "Europe", 
                     "Western Pacific", "Americas", 
                     "Eastern Mediterranean", "Africa")
  
  # check region only if provided
  if (!is.null(region)) {
    if (!is.character(region) || length(region) != 1) {
      stop("Region must be a single character string.")}
    
    if (!tolower(region) %in% tolower(valid_regions)) {
      stop("Invalid region. Options are: South East Asia, Europe, Western Pacific, Americas, Eastern Mediterranean, Africa.")}
    region <- str_to_title(region)}
  
  # check country only if provided
  if (!is.null(country)) {
    if (!is.character(country) || length(country) != 1) {
      stop("Country must be a single character string.")}
    
    if (!tolower(country) %in% tolower(measles$Country)) {
      stop("Country not found in dataset. Please check spelling.")}}
  
  # check year only if provided
  if (!is.null(year)) {
    if (!is.numeric(year) || length(year) != 1 || year %% 1 != 0) {
      stop("Year must be a single integer.")
    }
    if (!year %in% measles$Year) {
      warning("Year not found in dataset.")}}
  
  # given country and year
  if (!is.null(country) & !is.null(year)) {
    browsable(div(style = "max-height:500px; overflow-y:auto;", 
      measles |>
      dplyr::filter(Country == country, Year == year) |>
      dplyr::select(Country, `Nominal GDP per Capita`, 
                    `MCV1 Coverage`, `Total Measles per Million`) |>
        arrange(desc(`Total Measles per Million`)) |>
        rename("Nominal GDP per Capita ($ USD)" = `Nominal GDP per Capita`) |>
      gt() |>
      fmt_number(columns = c(`Nominal GDP per Capita ($ USD)`), decimals = 3) |>
      fmt(columns = `MCV1 Coverage`,
          fns = function(x) paste0(x, "%")) |>
      tab_header(title = md(paste("**Nominal GDP per Capita and MCV1 Coverage in **", year, "** For **", country))) |>
      tab_style(style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
                locations = cells_column_labels(everything()))))
    
  # given region and year
  } else if (!is.null(region) & !is.null(year)) {
    browsable(div(style = "max-height:500px; overflow-y:auto;", 
      measles |>
      dplyr::filter(Region == region, Year == year) |>
      dplyr::select(Country, `Nominal GDP per Capita`, 
                    `MCV1 Coverage`, `Total Measles per Million`) |>
        arrange(desc(`Total Measles per Million`)) |>
        rename("Nominal GDP per Capita ($ USD)" = `Nominal GDP per Capita`) |>
      gt() |>
      fmt_number(columns = c(`Nominal GDP per Capita ($ USD)`), decimals = 3) |>
      fmt(columns = `MCV1 Coverage`,
          fns = function(x) paste0(x, "%")) |>
      tab_header(title = md(glue::glue("**Nominal GDP per Capita and MCV1 Coverage in {year} for the  {region} Region**"))) |>
      tab_style(style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
                locations = cells_column_labels(everything()))))
    
  # given only country
  } else if (!is.null(country) & is.null(year)) {
    browsable(div(style = "max-height:500px; overflow-y:auto;", 
      measles |>
      dplyr::filter(Country == country) |>
      dplyr::select(Country, Year, `Nominal GDP per Capita`, 
                    `MCV1 Coverage`, `Total Measles per Million`) |>
        arrange(desc(`Total Measles per Million`)) |>
        rename("Nominal GDP per Capita ($ USD)" = `Nominal GDP per Capita`) |>
      gt() |>
      fmt_number(columns = c(`Nominal GDP per Capita ($ USD)`), decimals = 3) |>
      fmt(columns = `MCV1 Coverage`,
          fns = function(x) paste0(x, "%")) |>
      tab_header(title = md(glue::glue("**Nominal GDP per Capita and MCV1 Coverage for {country} Across Time**"))) |>
      tab_style(style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
                locations = cells_column_labels(everything()))) |>
      tab_footnote(footnote = "Scores are out of 100."))
    
  } else {
    stop("Invalid combination of inputs. Provide either country (+ optional year) or region + year.")}}
```

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
mcv_gdp(region = "western pacific", year = 2016)
```

We see in Table 4 that Mongolia had the most severe outbreak in recent history at close to 9500 cases per million in 2016. However, this population was 99% vaccinated with the first round of MCV. We continue to see relatively high rates in Malaysia, Singapore, and New Zealand despite MCV vaccination rate being above 90%.

## Statistical Model

**Process:**

Since the Total Number of Measles Cases can be modeled as count data, we first implemented a null Poisson generalized linear regression model. We aimed to maximize negative log-likelihood through the iterative addition of year-level predictor variables. 

While initially including population density, primary religion, nominal gdp per capita, region, year, as well as the measles vaccine coverage as our predictors, we found that religion and region were highly multicollinear according to their variance inflation factor, meaning most of the variability in total measles cases from religion could be explained by the region. 

Our final model included population density, nominal gdp per capita, region, year, and measles-containing vaccine first dose (MCV1) coverage, and implemented a Negative Binomial model rather than Poisson due to detected overdispersion.

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
# scaling and centering
model_data <- measles |>
  mutate(cPopDensity = scale(`Density`, scale = F),
         cNomGDP = scale(`Nominal GDP per Capita`, scale = F),
         cYear = scale(Year, scale = F),
         cMCV1 = scale(`MCV1 Coverage`, scale = F))
```

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#| warning: false
#| eval: false

# candidate models
# null model
m0 <- glm(`Total Measles` ~ 1, 
          family = poisson(link = "log"), 
          data = model_data)

# model 1
m1 <- glm(`Total Measles` ~ cPopDensity + `Primary Religion` + 
            cNomGDP + Region + cYear + cMCV1, 
          family = poisson(link = "log"), 
          data = model_data)

# model 2
m2 <- glm(`Total Measles` ~ cPopDensity + Region + cYear + cMCV1, 
          family = poisson(link = "log"), 
          data = model_data)

# model 3
m3 <- zeroinfl(`Total Measles` ~ cPopDensity + Region + cMCV1, 
               dist = "poisson", 
               data = model_data)

# model 4
m4 <- glm(`Total Measles` ~ cPopDensity + cNomGDP + Region + 
          cYear + cMCV1, 
          family = poisson(link = "log"), 
          data = model_data)

# model 5
m5 <- glm.nb(`Total Measles` ~ cPopDensity + cNomGDP + Region + 
               cYear + cMCV1, 
             data = model_data)

# overdispersion detected
odTest(m5)
```

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#| message: false
#| eval: false

# summaries

# null model
tidy(m0) |>
  gt()

# model 2
tidy(m1) |>
  gt()

# model 2
tidy(m2) |>
  gt()

# model 3 (no tidy() method for zero infl models)
summary(m3) 

# model 4
tidy(m4) |>
  gt()

# model 5
tidy(m5) |>
  gt()
```

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#| eval: false
# log likelihoods

# model 1
logLik(m1)

# model 2
logLik(m2)

# model 3
logLik(m3)

# model 4
logLik(m4)

# model 5
logLik(m5)
```

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#| eval: false
# vifs

# model 1
vif(m1)

# model 2
vif(m2)

# model 4
vif(m4)
```

#### **Table 5:** Negative Binomial Model Summary Table

```{r}
#| label: final model
#| code-fold: true

# final model
final_model <- glm.nb(`Total Measles` ~ cPopDensity + cNomGDP + 
                      Region + cYear + cMCV1, 
                      data = model_data)

final_model |>
  tidy() |>
  
  # clean up values
  mutate(term = str_replace_all(term, c("Region" = "",
                                        "cPopDensity" = "Pop. Density*",
                                        "cNomGDP" = "Nominal GDP per Capita*",
                                        "cYear" = "Year*",
                                        "cMCV1" = "MCV1 Coverage*",
                                        "\\(|\\)" = ""))) |>
  
  # rename values
  rename("Model Term" = "term",
         "Estimate" = "estimate",
         "Std. Error" = "std.error",
         "Z-Statistic" = "statistic",
         "p-value" = "p.value") |>
  
  # format table
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(decimals = 3) |>
  tab_header(title = md("**NB GLM Coefficients**")) |>
  tab_style(style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
            locations = cells_column_labels(everything())) |>
  
  # add caption
  tab_caption("Table 5: Negative Binomial regression results. Response measures total measles cases across a region in a given year. Baseline region is Africa.") |>
  
  # add footnote
  tab_footnote("*Quantitative variables are centered for interpretability")
```

**Interpretation:**

Based on the results of model fitting, we found that:

-   Southeast Asia has the greatest mean number of measles cases annually after adjusting for population density, nominal GDP per capita, year, and MCV1 coverage. 

-   The Americas have the lowest mean number of measles cases annually after adjusting for population density, nominal GDP per capita, year, and MCV1 coverage. 

-   Population density has a negative effect on mean measles cases after adjusting for the other variables, while nominal GDP per capita has a small negative effect on mean measles cases after adjusting for the other variables. 

-   Increases in year are associated with an increase in the mean number of measles cases after adjusting for the other variables. 

-   MCV1 coverage, or vaccination rate, has the largest negative effect on mean measles cases after adjusting for the other variables.
